questions.question: How is food security ensured in a country? Explain with its components.
questions.answer: Food security is ensured only if enough food is available for all persons, all persons have the capacity to buy food of acceptable quality, and there is no barrier on access to food. This involves three components: Availability (production + imports + stock), Accessibility (reachability), and Affordability (purchasing power).

questions.question: Which are the people more prone to food insecurity in India?
questions.answer: The most prone groups include landless laborers, traditional artisans, and petty self-employed workers in rural areas. In urban areas, casual laborers and those in ill-paid occupations are vulnerable. Socially, SCs, STs, and some sections of OBCs with low land-base or low productivity are highly insecure. Pregnant and nursing mothers and children under five also fall into this category.

questions.question: Differentiate between Chronic Hunger and Seasonal Hunger.
questions.answer: Chronic hunger is a consequence of diets persistently inadequate in terms of quantity/quality, often due to very low income. Seasonal hunger is related to cycles of food growing and harvesting. It is prevalent in rural areas due to the seasonal nature of agriculture and in urban areas for casual laborers who find less work during specific months.

questions.question: What has our government done to provide food security to the poor?
questions.answer: The government has designed a food security system composed of two main elements: (1) Buffer Stock and (2) Public Distribution System (PDS). Additionally, it has launched targeted schemes like Antyodaya Anna Yojana (AAY) and Annapurna Scheme (APS) linked with the existing network of PDS.

questions.question: Explain the role of the Food Corporation of India (FCI) in ensuring food security.
questions.answer: The FCI is the nodal agency that purchases wheat and rice from farmers in surplus states at the Minimum Support Price (MSP). It maintains the Buffer Stock and ensures that grains are transported from surplus regions to deficit regions for distribution through the PDS network.

questions.question: What is Minimum Support Price (MSP) and how does it benefit farmers?
questions.answer: MSP is the pre-announced price at which the government buys crops from farmers. It provides an incentive and a safety net to farmers by guaranteeing a price for their produce before the sowing season, protecting them from price fluctuations in the open market.

questions.question: Analyze the impact of the Green Revolution on India's food security.
questions.answer: The Green Revolution in the late 1960s, led by HYV seeds for wheat and rice, turned India from a food-deficient to a food-surplus nation. It enabled the government to build massive buffer stocks, ensuring that the country has not faced a famine-like situation even during adverse weather conditions.

questions.question: What are the problems with the current functioning of Ration Shops (Fair Price Shops)?
questions.answer: Problems include dealers diverting grains to the open market for better profit, selling poor-quality grains at ration shops, and irregular opening of the shops. Additionally, the accumulation of unsold stocks of poor-quality grain often leads to wastage.

questions.question: Explain the significance of the National Food Security Act, 2013.
questions.answer: The NFSA 2013 provides a legal right to food and nutritional security. It covers 75% of the rural and 50% of the urban population. Eligible persons are entitled to receive 5 kg of foodgrains per person per month at highly subsidized prices (₹3 for rice, ₹2 for wheat, and ₹1 for coarse grains).

questions.question: How do cooperatives play a role in food security in India? Give examples.
questions.answer: Cooperatives set up shops to sell controlled goods at reasonable prices. For example, in Tamil Nadu, 94% of fair price shops are run by cooperatives. Mother Dairy in Delhi provides milk and vegetables at controlled rates. Amul in Gujarat has ensured milk security through its network.

questions.question: Discuss the 'Antyodaya Anna Yojana' (AAY) and its target group.
questions.answer: Launched in 2000, AAY targets one crore of the 'poorest of the poor' among the BPL families. It provides 35 kg of foodgrains to each family at highly subsidized rates (₹2/kg wheat, ₹3/kg rice). It is one of the most successful targeted anti-poverty programs.

questions.question: What is 'Issue Price' and why is it important?
questions.answer: Issue Price is the price at which the government distributes grains from the buffer stock to the poor through ration shops. It is important because it is much lower than the market price, making food grains affordable for the BPL and Antyodaya families.

questions.question: Describe the three-pronged attack on poverty through food-based programs in the 1970s.
questions.answer: In the mid-1970s, three major programs were introduced: (1) Public Distribution System (PDS) for foodgrains, (2) Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS), and (3) Food for Work (FFW). These targeted the immediate nutritional needs of the poor.

questions.question: How does a drought-like situation lead to starvation?
questions.answer: A drought causes a decline in total food production, leading to a shortage. This results in high prices. Those without purchasing power cannot buy food, leading to widespread malnutrition and, if prolonged, starvation deaths.

questions.question: What are the advantages of maintaining a Buffer Stock?
questions.answer: Buffer stock (1) helps in distributing foodgrains in deficit areas, (2) provides food to the poorer strata at subsidized rates, and (3) helps resolve food shortage problems during adverse weather conditions or periods of calamity.

questions.question: Explain the role of the Academy of Development Science (ADS) in Maharashtra.
questions.answer: ADS has facilitated a network of NGOs for setting up grain banks in different regions. It provides training on food security for NGOs and successfully influences the government's food security policy by demonstrating the grain bank model for tribal communities.

questions.question: Why is the PDS being criticized in recent years?
questions.answer: Criticism centers on (1) overflowing granaries while people still go hungry, (2) high carrying costs for the government, (3) diversion of grains by dealers, and (4) the exclusion of many needy people due to targeting errors (APL/BPL classification).

questions.question: What is the 'Subsidy' and how does it relate to food prices in India?
questions.answer: A subsidy is a payment that a government makes to a producer or supplier to keep the price of a commodity low. In India, the government subsidizes food by buying at high MSP and selling at low Issue Price, covering the gap through the national budget.

questions.question: Analyze the geographical distribution of food insecurity in India.
questions.answer: Food-insecure people are disproportionately large in economically backward states with high poverty like Bihar, Odisha, Uttar Pradesh, West Bengal, Chhattisgarh, and parts of Madhya Pradesh and Maharashtra. This is often linked to low agricultural productivity and high disaster vulnerability.

questions.question: How did India become self-sufficient in food grains?
questions.answer: Self-sufficiency was achieved through the Green Revolution, expanding irrigation, using fertilizers, and adopting HYV seeds. The government's support through MSP and procurement by FCI ensured that farmers had an incentive to produce more.

questions.question: What is the significance of the 1943 Bengal Famine in the study of food security?
questions.answer: The Bengal Famine killed 30 lakh people and highlighted that food insecurity isn't just about availability; it's about access. It showed how a sudden rise in prices and administrative failure can lead to mass starvation even without a complete crop failure.

questions.question: How do 'Grain Banks' help in providing food security?
questions.answer: Grain banks allow community members to deposit surplus grain and borrow during lean seasons or calamities. This localized system reduces dependency on distant government ration shops and provides an immediate safety net for tribal and remote communities.

questions.question: Explain the term 'Marketable Surplus'.
questions.answer: Marketable surplus is the portion of agricultural produce which is sold in the market by the farmers after meeting their own consumption requirements. This surplus is what the FCI procures for the national buffer stock.

questions.question: Compare the Targeted Public Distribution System (TPDS) with the earlier Universal PDS.
questions.answer: Universal PDS provided subsidized grains to everyone. TPDS, introduced in 1997, divided the population into APL and BPL, focusing higher subsidies on the BPL category. While TPDS aims to save money, it is criticized for excluding needy people due to classification errors.

questions.question: What is the role of the 'Annapurna Scheme' (APS)?
questions.answer: APS targets 'indigent senior citizens' who are eligible for the National Old Age Pension Scheme but are not receiving it. It provides 10 kg of foodgrains per month free of cost to ensure their nutritional security.
